Dashboard integration is a game-changer for data-driven decision-making. It enables organizations to connect diverse data sources into a unified view for insights. But here’s the catch: many businesses overlook the importance of real-time data integration, thinking static reports are sufficient. In reality, **organizations utilizing real-time dashboards see a significant boost of up to 37% in user participation**. This raises a critical question—how can you leverage integration strategies to not only foster collaboration but also enhance the speed of your operations?

Dashboard Integration: Key Concepts
-----------------------------------

![Image 4: Hands integrating data on tech devices](https://csuxjmfbwmkxiegfpljm.supabase.co/storage/v1/object/public/blog-images/hands_integrating_data_on_tech_devices_SXGiIxR848B96pDNNl9GZ.png)

Dashboard integration is more than just connecting data sources—it’s about creating a cohesive ecosystem where information flows seamlessly to enable better decision-making. Understanding the fundamental concepts behind effective dashboard integration strategies is essential for organizations looking to maximize their data’s value.

### Interoperability: The Foundation of Integration

[![Image 5: Making a Digital Dashboard! (w/ Google Calendar integration)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/BR_yko0gr-Y/maxresdefault.jpg?sqp=-oaymwEQCJQBEFPyq4qpAwVwAbgCAQ&rs=AMzJL3musb8xNU4VNPEFEHATLo_oqQ5i_A)](https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=BR_yko0gr-Y&t=111s)

At the core of successful dashboard integration is interoperability—the ability for dashboard systems and applications to work seamlessly across platforms. According to [Superblocks Integration Strategy Team](https://www.superblocks.com/blog/digital-integration-strategy), “Success depends on how you design, manage, and evolve your integrations over time. Prioritizing interoperability and automation, while standardizing on open APIs and data formats, helps set integration efforts up for long-term impact.”

Interoperability ensures your dashboards can easily pull from and push data to multiple sources without extensive manual intervention. This capability becomes increasingly important as organizations expand their tech stacks and data sources.

Best practices for dashboard integration in 2025 emphasize choosing platforms with open, well-documented APIs—such as REST or GraphQL—and supporting common data formats like JSON, XML, or CSV. These choices enable scalable interoperability across systems and future-proof your integration investments.

### Component Libraries: Building Blocks for Consistency

Component libraries serve as collections of pre-designed UI elements and visualization templates that can be reused across different dashboards. These standardized building blocks play a crucial role in maintaining consistency while accelerating development.

Organizations using component libraries in their dashboard development process report [reducing development time by up to 60%](https://bricxlabs.com/blogs/tips-for-dashboard-design), highlighting the significant efficiency gains through standardized visual components. This standardization not only speeds up initial development but also simplifies ongoing maintenance and updates.

A well-implemented component library ensures that:

*   Visual elements maintain consistency across all integrated dashboards
*   Data visualizations follow best practices for clarity and comprehension
*   Branding elements appear correctly throughout the ecosystem
*   New dashboard features can be rapidly deployed using proven components

### Automation: Streamlining Integration Workflows

Automation has become a cornerstone of effective dashboard integration. The adoption rate of automation tools for routine dashboard integration tasks has accelerated, with [over 65% of organizations deploying automation workflows](https://www.superblocks.com/blog/digital-integration-strategy) in 2024–2025, up from 48% in 2022.

By automating repetitive integration tasks like data syncing and status updates using tools such as iPaaS or app-building platforms, organizations reduce manual errors and increase operational efficiency. This allows teams to focus on strategic initiatives rather than mundane data management tasks.

Effective automation in dashboard integration includes:

1.   Scheduled data refreshes that maintain dashboard currency without manual intervention
2.   Alert systems that notify users of significant data changes or threshold breaches
3.   Workflow triggers that initiate actions based on dashboard metrics
4.   Cross-platform data synchronization that ensures consistency across systems

A common misconception is that [dashboards must be built on proprietary or closed systems for better security and reliability](https://www.superblocks.com/blog/digital-integration-strategy). In reality, open, well-documented APIs and standardized data formats increase security through vetted protocols and allow for more scalable, reliable integrations—especially when combined with thoughtful automation.

Understanding these key concepts provides the foundation for implementing effective dashboard integration strategies that will scale with your organization’s needs and enable data-driven decision making across departments.

Real-Time Data Integration Benefits
-----------------------------------

Real-time data integration represents a significant evolution in dashboard integration strategies. By connecting your dashboards to live data sources, you unlock powerful capabilities that transform how your organization makes decisions and responds to changing conditions.

### Accelerated Decision-Making

When dashboards pull data in real time, decision-makers no longer need to wait for scheduled reports or manual updates. This immediacy translates directly into faster, more responsive business operations.

Modern dashboard integration strategies are increasingly moving toward event-driven architectures, [leveraging real-time data updates through webhooks, pub/sub models, and automated triggers](https://www.superblocks.com/blog/digital-integration-strategy). These approaches significantly enhance dashboard responsiveness and keep data fresh, enabling organizations to spot trends, identify issues, and capitalize on opportunities the moment they arise.

Consider a marketing campaign dashboard that updates in real time. Marketing teams can immediately see which channels are performing well and adjust spend accordingly, rather than discovering this information hours or days later when the opportunity to optimize has passed.

### Enhanced Operational Visibility

Real-time data integration provides unprecedented visibility into current operations. For businesses requiring operational dashboards, this translates to continuous awareness of system performance, customer interactions, and business processes as they happen.

Organizations can use open APIs and automation workflows to [connect dashboards with real-time data across CRM, ERP, and cloud platforms](https://www.superblocks.com/blog/digital-integration-strategy), gaining faster and more accurate business insights. This integration breaks down traditional data silos, creating a unified view of operations that previously required piecing together information from multiple sources.

For example, a customer service dashboard with real-time integration can display current call volumes, average resolution times, and customer satisfaction scores as they evolve throughout the day. This allows managers to allocate resources proactively rather than reactively.

### Proactive Problem Resolution

Perhaps one of the most valuable benefits of real-time dashboard integration is the ability to identify and address problems before they escalate into major issues.

By setting up alert thresholds and monitoring key metrics in real time, organizations can create early warning systems that flag potential problems. These proactive notifications enable teams to investigate and resolve issues before customers are impacted or before they grow into costly problems.

For example, an e-commerce platform with real-time dashboard integration might detect an unusual spike in cart abandonment rates, allowing the team to quickly investigate and fix a payment processing issue before significant revenue is lost.

### Competitive Advantage Through Agility

In today’s fast-paced business environment, agility is often the difference between market leaders and followers. Real-time dashboard integration provides the informational foundation for this agility.

Organizations that can access, analyze, and act on real-time data gain a significant competitive advantage. They can:

*   Respond to market changes more quickly than competitors
*   Identify emerging customer needs as they develop
*   Optimize operations continuously rather than periodically
*   Test and iterate on strategies with immediate feedback

The business value of real-time integration becomes particularly evident when examining user engagement. Dashboards offering tailored customization options and real-time data updates see [up to a 37% increase in user participation](https://bricxlabs.com/blogs/tips-for-dashboard-design), underscoring the value of user-centric design combined with timely information.

By integrating real-time data into your dashboards, you transform them from passive reporting tools into active decision-support systems. This shift fundamentally changes how teams interact with data and ultimately how organizations operate in response to changing conditions.

The investment in real-time dashboard integration typically pays dividends through faster response times, better decision-making, and the ability to seize opportunities that might otherwise be missed with traditional batch processing approaches.

Implementing Best Integration Practices
---------------------------------------

Successful dashboard integration requires careful planning and execution. By following established best practices, you can avoid common pitfalls and create dashboard systems that deliver lasting value. Let’s explore the most effective approaches to implementing dashboard integration strategies.

![Image 6: Professional analyzes dashboard performance](https://csuxjmfbwmkxiegfpljm.supabase.co/storage/v1/object/public/blog-images/professional_analyzes_dashboard_performance_Y5lejqv7cJCL8Pp3_Yxuk.png)

### Start With Clear Business Requirements

Before diving into technical implementation, clearly define what your integrated dashboard needs to accomplish. This starts with identifying key stakeholders and understanding their specific information needs.

Gather requirements by asking questions like:

*   What decisions will users make based on dashboard data?
*   Which metrics are critical for each user role?
*   How frequently does data need to update?
*   What integrations with existing systems are necessary?

[Fuselab Creative Team](https://fuselabcreative.com/top-dashboard-design-trends-2025/) emphasizes that “User feedback is critical throughout the process. Regular testing and iteration are essential to ensure that the dashboard meets the needs of users and provides the desired insights.”

This iterative approach to dashboard development ensures the final product aligns with business requirements and user needs, maximizing usability and adoption.

### Prioritize Open, Standards-Based Integration

When selecting tools and platforms for dashboard integration, prioritize those with open APIs and support for standard data formats. This approach maximizes flexibility and future-proofs your integration investments.

Best practices for dashboard integration in 2025 emphasize choosing platforms with open, well-documented APIs—such as REST or GraphQL—and supporting common data formats like JSON, XML, or CSV. These standards enable scalable interoperability across systems.

A common misconception is that proprietary systems provide better security and reliability. In reality, open standards often provide more robust security through community validation and easier integration with your existing security infrastructure.

### Implement Thoughtful Data Architecture

Effective dashboard integration depends on a well-designed data architecture. Consider how data will flow between systems and how it will be transformed to meet dashboard requirements.

Key elements of good data architecture include:

1.   Data warehousing or lake strategies to consolidate information
2.   ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) processes to prepare data for visualization
3.   Caching mechanisms to optimize performance
4.   Clear data governance for accuracy and security

Increasingly, organizations are moving toward event-driven architectures for dashboard integration. This approach [leverages real-time data updates through webhooks, pub/sub models, and automated triggers](https://www.superblocks.com/blog/digital-integration-strategy), enhancing responsiveness and ensuring data freshness.

### Leverage Component Libraries for Consistency

Component libraries dramatically improve both the efficiency and quality of dashboard implementations. These standardized building blocks ensure visual consistency while accelerating development.

Organizations using component libraries can [reduce their dashboard development time by up to 60%](https://bricxlabs.com/blogs/tips-for-dashboard-design). This efficiency comes from avoiding redundant work and leveraging pre-tested, optimized visualization components.

When implementing component libraries:

*   Select libraries that align with your brand guidelines
*   Ensure components work across all target devices and screen sizes
*   Document standards for component usage to maintain consistency
*   Train your team on proper implementation of the component system

### Automate Integration Workflows

Automation is essential for maintaining efficient dashboard integrations over time. By automating repetitive tasks like data syncing and status updates, you reduce manual errors and free your team to focus on higher-value activities.

The adoption of automation for dashboard integration has accelerated, with over 65% of organizations now deploying automation workflows. This trend reflects the significant efficiency gains from automated integration processes.

Implement automation for:

*   Regular data synchronization between systems
*   Validation checks to ensure data quality
*   Alert generation when metrics cross thresholds
*   Dashboard refresh schedules aligned with business needs

### Test, Measure, and Iterate

Dashboard integration is never truly “finished.” The most successful implementations include processes for continuous improvement based on user feedback and performance metrics.

Regularly measure dashboard usage, load times, and user satisfaction. Use these metrics to identify opportunities for improvement and prioritize enhancements.

According to [BRICX Labs](https://bricxlabs.com/blogs/tips-for-dashboard-design), “Smooth brand integration makes dashboard recognition stronger while keeping professional esthetics.” This perspective highlights the importance of not just functional integration but also cohesive visual design that reinforces organizational identity.

By following these best practices, you’ll create dashboard integrations that are technically sound, user-friendly, and aligned with your business objectives. Remember that successful integration is an ongoing process—one that evolves as your organization’s needs and capabilities grow.

Overcoming Common Integration Challenges
----------------------------------------

Implementing dashboard integration strategies inevitably involves navigating various obstacles. Understanding and preparing for these challenges can dramatically improve your chances of success. Let’s explore the most common integration hurdles and practical approaches to overcome them.

### Data Quality and Consistency Issues

Poor data quality presents one of the most persistent challenges in dashboard integration. When dashboards pull from multiple sources, inconsistencies in data formats, naming conventions, and update frequencies can create misleading visualizations and erode user trust.

To address data quality challenges:

*   Implement data validation rules at integration points to catch and flag inconsistencies
*   Create a data dictionary that standardizes definitions across all integrated systems
*   Establish clear data ownership and governance policies
*   Build automated data quality monitoring into your integration workflows

Remember that dashboard integrations are only as valuable as the data they present. Investing in data quality processes pays dividends through more accurate insights and higher user confidence.

### Technical Compatibility Limitations

Integrating dashboards with legacy systems, proprietary platforms, or incompatible technologies often creates significant technical hurdles. These compatibility issues can delay implementation and compromise functionality.

Effective strategies for overcoming technical compatibility challenges include:

1.   Deploying middleware or API gateways to bridge incompatible systems
2.   Using ETL tools specifically designed for data integration scenarios
3.   Creating custom connectors when standard integration methods aren’t available
4.   Implementing data warehousing to normalize data from diverse systems

A practical approach used by successful organizations involves [involving end-users in iterative feedback cycles when designing integrations](https://fuselabcreative.com/top-dashboard-design-trends-2025/). This ensures the dashboard evolves to meet actual needs—maximizing value and encouraging organizational adoption despite technical limitations.

### Performance Bottlenecks

Dashboard performance issues often emerge as integration complexity increases. Slow loading times and delayed data updates can frustrate users and diminish the value of real-time insights.

To address performance challenges:

*   Implement strategic data caching to reduce query loads
*   Optimize database queries that feed dashboard visualizations
*   Consider asynchronous data loading for non-critical elements
*   Monitor performance metrics and establish alerting for degradations
*   Scale infrastructure resources to match dashboard usage patterns

A common misconception is that [more features and complexity always result in a better dashboard](https://bricxlabs.com/blogs/tips-for-dashboard-design). In reality, effective dashboards prioritize user needs and simplicity. Overcomplication often leads to performance issues, lower adoption, and confusion among users.

### Security and Access Control Complexity

Integrated dashboards frequently access sensitive data from multiple systems, creating complex security requirements. Balancing accessibility with proper protection of confidential information presents a significant challenge.

Best practices for addressing security challenges include:

*   Implementing role-based access controls that limit data visibility based on user permissions
*   Encrypting data both in transit and at rest throughout the integration pipeline
*   Conducting regular security audits of dashboard integrations
*   Using single sign-on (SSO) solutions to streamline secure access
*   Creating data anonymization processes for sensitive information when appropriate

Consistent security implementation across integrated systems is crucial. Security controls should be documented clearly and reviewed regularly as new data sources are added.

### Organizational Resistance and Adoption Issues

Even technically flawless dashboard integrations can fail if users resist adoption or departments are reluctant to share data. Addressing these human factors is often as important as solving technical challenges.

Strategies for overcoming organizational resistance include:

*   Involving stakeholders early in the integration planning process
*   Providing comprehensive training on how to use integrated dashboards
*   Demonstrating clear value through relevant use cases for each department
*   Creating internal champions who promote dashboard adoption
*   Establishing feedback mechanisms to continuously improve the user experience

Dashboards offering tailored customization options see [up to a 37% increase in user participation](https://bricxlabs.com/blogs/tips-for-dashboard-design), underscoring the importance of user-centric design in overcoming adoption challenges.

### Maintenance and Scalability Concerns

Many organizations underestimate the ongoing effort required to maintain dashboard integrations, especially as business needs evolve and data sources change. Planning for maintenance and future growth is essential for long-term success.

To address maintenance and scalability challenges:

*   Document all integration points thoroughly
*   Create monitoring systems that detect integration failures
*   Establish change management processes for updating connected systems
*   Design modular integration architecture that accommodates new data sources
*   Plan for regular reviews and updates to maintain alignment with business needs

By anticipating these common challenges and implementing thoughtful solutions, you can create dashboard integrations that deliver lasting value across your organization. Remember that successful integration is as much about people and processes as it is about technology.

Frequently Asked Questions
--------------------------

#### What is dashboard integration?

Dashboard integration refers to the process of connecting diverse data sources into a unified dashboard view, enabling organizations to gain insights for data-driven decision-making.

#### Why is real-time data integration important for dashboards?

Real-time data integration is crucial as it allows decision-makers to access live data, facilitating faster decision-making, enhancing operational visibility, and enabling proactive problem resolution.

#### What are best practices for implementing dashboard integration?

Key best practices include starting with clear business requirements, prioritizing open standards-based integration, implementing thoughtful data architecture, utilizing component libraries for consistency, automating workflows, and regularly testing and iterating based on user feedback.

#### How can organizations overcome common integration challenges?

Organizations can address common challenges such as data quality issues, technical compatibility limitations, and user resistance by implementing data validation processes, using middleware for compatibility, and involving stakeholders early in the integration process.

# Grafana dashboard best practices

This section provides information about best practices for intermediate Grafana administrators and users about how to build and maintain Grafana dashboards.

For more information about the different kinds of dashboards you can create, refer to [Grafana dashboards: A complete guide to all the different types you can build](/blog/2022/06/06/grafana-dashboards-a-complete-guide-to-all-the-different-types-you-can-build/?pg=webinar-getting-started-with-grafana-dashboard-design-amer&plcmt=related-content-1).

## Common observability strategies

When you have a lot to monitor, like a server farm, you need a strategy to decide what is important enough to monitor. This page describes several common methods for choosing what to monitor.

A logical strategy allows you to make uniform dashboards and scale your observability platform more easily.

### Guidelines for usage

### USE method

USE stands for:

This method is best for hardware resources in infrastructure, such as CPU, memory, and network devices. For more information, refer to [The USE Method](http://www.brendangregg.com/usemethod.html).

### RED method

RED stands for:

This method is most applicable to services, especially a microservices environment. For each of your services, instrument the code to expose these metrics for each component. RED dashboards are good for alerting and SLAs. A well-designed RED dashboard is a proxy for user experience.

For more information, refer to Tom Wilkie’s blog post [The RED method: How to instrument your services](/blog/2018/08/02/the-red-method-how-to-instrument-your-services).

### The Four Golden Signals

According to the [Google SRE handbook](https://landing.google.com/sre/sre-book/chapters/monitoring-distributed-systems/#xref_monitoring_golden-signals), if you can only measure four metrics of your user-facing system, focus on these four.

This method is similar to the RED method, but it includes saturation.

![Give it a try using Grafana Play]()

With Grafana Play, you can explore and see how it works, learning from practical examples to accelerate your development.
This feature can be seen on [The Four Golden Signals](https://play.grafana.org/d/000000109/).

## Dashboard management maturity model

*Dashboard management maturity* refers to how well-designed and efficient your dashboard ecosystem is. It’s recommended that you periodically review your dashboard setup to gauge where you are and how you can improve.

Broadly speaking, dashboard maturity can be defined as low, medium, or high.

Much of the content for this topic was taken from the KubeCon 2019 talk [Fool-Proof Kubernetes Dashboards for Sleep-Deprived Oncalls](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YE2aQFiMGfY).

### Low - default state

At this stage, you have no coherent dashboard management strategy. Almost everyone starts here.

How can you tell you are here?

### Medium - methodical dashboards

At this stage, you are starting to manage your dashboard use with methodical dashboards. You might have laid out a strategy, but there are some things you could improve.

How can you tell you are here?

Prevent sprawl by using template variables. For example, you don’t need a separate dashboard for each node, you can use query variables. Even better, you can make the data source a template variable too, so you can reuse the same dashboard across different clusters and monitoring backends.

Refer to the list of
[Variable examples](/docs/grafana/latest/dashboards/variables/#examples-of-templates-and-variables) if you want some ideas.

Methodical dashboards according to an [observability strategy](#common-observability-strategies).

Hierarchical dashboards with drill-downs to the next level.

![Example of using drill-down]( "Example of using drill-down")
![Example of using drill-down](/static/img/docs/best-practices/drill-down-example.png "Example of using drill-down")

Dashboard design reflects service hierarchies. The example shown below uses the RED method (request and error rate on the left, latency duration on the right) with one row per service. The row order reflects the data flow.

![Example of a service hierarchy]( "Example of a service hierarchy")
![Example of a service hierarchy](/static/img/docs/best-practices/service-hierarchy-example.png "Example of a service hierarchy")

Compare like to like: split service dashboards when the magnitude differs. Make sure aggregated metrics don’t drown out important information.

Expressive charts with meaningful use of color and normalizing axes where you can.

Directed browsing cuts down on “guessing.”

Version-controlled dashboard JSON.

### High - optimized use

At this stage, you have optimized your dashboard management use with a consistent and thoughtful strategy. It requires maintenance, but the results are worth it.

## Best practices for creating dashboards

This page outlines some best practices to follow when creating Grafana dashboards.

### Before you begin

Here are some principles to consider before you create a dashboard.

#### A dashboard should tell a story or answer a question

What story are you trying to tell with your dashboard? Try to create a logical progression of data, such as large to small or general to specific. What is the goal for this dashboard? (Hint: If the dashboard doesn’t have a goal, then ask yourself if you really need the dashboard.)

Keep your graphs simple and focused on answering the question that you are asking. For example, if your question is “which servers are in trouble?”, then maybe you don’t need to show all the server data. Just show data for the ones in trouble.

#### Dashboards should reduce cognitive load, not add to it

*Cognitive load* is basically how hard you need to think about something in order to figure it out. Make your dashboard easy to interpret. Other users and future you (when you’re trying to figure out what broke at 2 AM) will appreciate it.

Ask yourself:

#### Have a monitoring strategy

It’s easy to make new dashboards. It’s harder to optimize dashboard creation and adhere to a plan, but it’s worth it. This strategy should govern both your overall dashboard scheme and enforce consistency in individual dashboard design.

Refer to [Common observability strategies](#common-observability-strategies) and [Dashboard management maturity levels](#dashboard-management-maturity-model) for more information.

#### Write it down

Once you have a strategy or design guidelines, write them down to help maintain consistency over time. Check out this [Wikimedia runbook example](https://wikitech.wikimedia.org/wiki/Performance/Runbook/Grafana_best_practices).

## Best practices for managing dashboards

This page outlines some best practices to follow when managing Grafana dashboards.

### Before you begin

Here are some principles to consider before you start managing dashboards.

#### Strategic observability

There are several [common observability strategies](#common-observability-strategies). You should research them and decide whether one of them works for you or if you want to come up with your own. Either way, have a plan, write it down, and stick to it.

Adapt your strategy to changing needs as necessary.

#### Maturity level

What is your dashboard maturity level? Analyze your current dashboard setup and compare it to the [Dashboard management maturity model](#dashboard-management-maturity-model). Understanding where you are can help you decide how to get to where you want to be.

Guidelines and Best Practices for Developing Dashboards
=======================================================

NEC Security Blog

Jul 5, 2024

[※日本語版はこちら（For the Japanese version, please refer to here.）](https://jpn.nec.com/cybersecurity/blog/240705/index.html?teal_com=0198625dfcb700125b78eeeaf23c05075004c06d00590)

SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) is often used in many situations for cybersecurity monitoring to efficiently analyze data collected from various sensors. Additionally, in the analysis of security incidents, a log analysis platform is used to analyze logs from multiple devices. Dashboards are commonly utilized in these situations. By visualizing analysis contents in a dashboard in advance, analysts can easily access the information they need, which leads to enhanced analysis efficiency and improved analysis quality.

On May 31, 2024, the Digital Agency, Government of Japan released the "Dashboard Design Practice Guidebook and Chart Component Library (Beta)" (referred to as the “Practical Guidebook”).[![Image 3](https://www.nec.com/nf_global/v3/common/img/icn/_blank.png)[1]](https://www.digital.go.jp/resources/dashboard-guidebook) This document aims to enable efficient development of simple dashboards by systematizing the process and development. While the guidebook is intended for use with the business analysis tool "Power BI [![Image 4](https://www.nec.com/nf_global/v3/common/img/icn/_blank.png)[2]](https://www.microsoft.com/ja-jp/power-platform/products/power-bi)," many of the ideas outlined in it can also be applied to the development of dashboards for cybersecurity monitoring and security incident situations.

In this blog, I would like to introduce the Practical Guidebook as well as additional resources to supplement it.

Development Process Advocated by the Practical Guidebook
--------------------------------------------------------

The Practical Guidebook divides the dashboard development process into three stages.

1.   Requirements Definition 
2.   Prototyping 
3.   Implementation 

[![Image 5](https://www.nec.com/en/global/solutions/cybersecurity/blog/240705/images/img01_s.jpg)![Image 6: zoom](https://www.nec.com/nf_global/v3/common/img/icn/zoom.png)Larger view](https://www.nec.com/en/global/solutions/cybersecurity/blog/240705/images/img01.jpg)

**Figure-1: The flow of dashboards development（Excerpted from [![Image 7](https://www.nec.com/nf_global/v3/common/img/icn/_blank.png)[1]](https://www.digital.go.jp/resources/dashboard-guidebook)）**

In the requirements definition step, the 5W1H framework is used for organizing the information. By using this framework, it defines the radical purpose of the dashboard. Also, it helps to clarify what information to provide for viewers and what actions they should take after viewing the dashboard. The "Requirements Definition Worksheet" that can be used for organizing those ideas will be very helpful for considering the story to tell the viewers of the dashboard. In particular, the “purpose of viewing” which is organized by "What, so What" is also an important factor in considering what kind of information needs to be displayed on the dashboard.

In the prototyping step, in order to achieve the content organized in the requirements definition, a cycle of data organization, prototype creation, and feedback gathering is used. With the term “prototype,” many people will think of creating something that actually works and making minor modifications based on feedback. Regarding prototypes advocated in practical guidebooks, terms such as “wireframe” and “mock-up” are easier to convey the intention. Based on the results of data organization, the layout of the framework (wireframe) is examined, the visualization items such as charts, graphs, and diagrams are arranged and colored according to the framework (mock-up), and then feedback is reflected to reconcile the images. This can be done by using tools such as PowerPoint or other office software, which allow for quick and easy creation and modification. By following this, it can help reduce the risk of costly rework later.

In the implementation step, by using the pre-defined parts of Power BI envisioned in the Practical Guidebook, it is possible to proceed with development with a unified design (Chart component library). When using a dashboard development platform other than Power BI, having the parts of unified design and setting would enable a consistent development style. Additionally, it provides a "checklist" to recheck whether the requirements identified in the requirement definition have been adequately implemented (This checklist is available in “5.4 Checklist” in the Practical Guidebook, or in the “Related Materials of the Practical Guidebook” in [![Image 8](https://www.nec.com/nf_global/v3/common/img/icn/_blank.png)[1]](https://www.digital.go.jp/resources/dashboard-guidebook)). This checklist covers many aspects of design and content that are necessary for any dashboard development platform, so it will be useful for finalizing the dashboards.

Utilizing the Practical Guidebook in Dashboard Development Platform
-------------------------------------------------------------------

The Practical Guidebook is designed for Power BI, so in the case of other dashboard development platforms, it needs to be adjusted to its platform. In this section, I will introduce the materials provided by the business intelligence tool "Domo" to reinforce the Practical Guidebook. Also, I would like to share my idea of using the Practical Guidebook in Splunk Enterprise, where I have experience in dashboard development.

### The Dashboard Development Process

The same concept can be applied to the overall flow of the development process regardless of the dashboard development platform. In Splunk, a presentation on the best practice of dashboard development "Dashboard Design and Best Practices" was also released [![Image 9](https://www.nec.com/nf_global/v3/common/img/icn/_blank.png)[3]](https://conf.splunk.com/watch/conf-online.html?search=PLA1583C#/). This presentation also starts by clearly defining the goals and what kind of viewers and purpose the dashboard will be provided. Also, the contents were arranged in a notebook based on what has been considered and the composition of the panels was thought out. This approach is similar to the method introduced in the “prototyping.”

### Requirements Definition

The "Requirement Definition Worksheet" provided by the Practical Guidebook can be used to organize dashboard requirements in layers that are not affected by the dashboard development platform. When developing dashboards with Splunk Enterprise, using a worksheet at the beginning to clarify the requirements in terms of 5W1H will be helpful for later prototyping and implementation steps.

### Prototyping

The following process will involve considerations specific to each dashboard development platform.

In the data organization section in the Practical Guidebook, what kind of data is used and what visualization items are used are considered. While the Practical Guidebook provides ideas for choosing visualization items, the “Diagram of graph type” which is provided in the “Best Practices for Choosing Chart Types[![Image 10](https://www.nec.com/nf_global/v3/common/img/icn/_blank.png)[4]](https://domo-support.domo.com/s/article/360042935294)” in Domo, can be a powerful tool for selecting visualization items.

[![Image 11](https://www.nec.com/en/global/solutions/cybersecurity/blog/240705/images/img02_s.jpg)![Image 12: zoom](https://www.nec.com/nf_global/v3/common/img/icn/zoom.png)Larger view](https://www.nec.com/en/global/solutions/cybersecurity/blog/240705/images/img02.jpg)

**Figure-2: Diagram of graph type (Excerpted from [![Image 13](https://www.nec.com/nf_global/v3/common/img/icn/_blank.png)[4]](https://domo-support.domo.com/s/article/360042935294))**

For examining the layout as a framework using PowerPoint (wireframe), the same method as the Practical Guidebook can be followed. On the other hand, when creating a mock-up, the appearance of the visualization items that can be used in the dashboard development platform may differ from Power BI, there will be a deviation from the image during implementation if it is used as it is. Splunk Enterprise comes with the Examples Hub [![Image 14](https://www.nec.com/nf_global/v3/common/img/icn/_blank.png)[5]](https://docs.splunk.com/Documentation/Splunk/Latest/DashStudio/ExHub), a catalog of visualization items and dashboard examples that can be used within the system.

[![Image 15](https://www.nec.com/en/global/solutions/cybersecurity/blog/240705/images/img03_s.jpg)![Image 16: zoom](https://www.nec.com/nf_global/v3/common/img/icn/zoom.png)Larger view](https://www.nec.com/en/global/solutions/cybersecurity/blog/240705/images/img03.jpg)

**Figure-3: Splunk Examples Hub (Accessible from http(s)://your-splunk-server:port/en-US/app/splunk-dashboard-studio)**

The Examples Hub covers all of the visualization items available in Splunk Enterprise, including almost all patterns of the same visualization items that change its appearance with optional parameters. Using these parts will also make it easier to proceed with the mock-up creation.

### Implementation

The Examples Hub is also utilized for implementation. Even for complex visualization items, the source code is provided along with them. By simply copying and pasting the code, it is possible to get the same look implementation.

[![Image 17](https://www.nec.com/en/global/solutions/cybersecurity/blog/240705/images/img04_s.jpg)![Image 18: zoom](https://www.nec.com/nf_global/v3/common/img/icn/zoom.png)Larger view](https://www.nec.com/en/global/solutions/cybersecurity/blog/240705/images/img04.jpg)

**Figure-4: Sankey Diagram (Excerpted from Examples Hub)**

If you want to use a different color theme than the Examples Hub in your project, it would be a good idea to prepare the corresponding code in advance. Since the background color of the visualization items and the color used for items in the part are prepared with parameter names with common wording such as "backgroundColor" and "seriesColors", you can easily change the color theme of various visualization items just by having this code ready.

[![Image 19](https://www.nec.com/en/global/solutions/cybersecurity/blog/240705/images/img05_s.jpg)![Image 20: zoom](https://www.nec.com/nf_global/v3/common/img/icn/zoom.png)Larger view](https://www.nec.com/en/global/solutions/cybersecurity/blog/240705/images/img05.jpg)

**Figure-5: Changing background colors and color themes (Created based on code in Examples Hub)**

If you are unsure about how to name panel titles and other items, "Naming Conventions Best Practices [![Image 21](https://www.nec.com/nf_global/v3/common/img/icn/_blank.png)[6]](https://domo-support.domo.com/s/article/360042935534)” provided by Domo may give you some hints. It will provide guidance on how to name the titles that describe the content of the panel.

Similar to the requirements definition worksheet, the "checklist" provided by the Practical Guidebook can be used to check the implementation contents in a layer that is not affected by the dashboard development platform. While there are some items that may be difficult to achieve in the dashboard development platforms such as some layout-related items, most of the items will be helpful during the final touch-up stages of dashboards.

Conclusion
----------

In this article, I have introduced the "Dashboard Design Guidebook" published by the Digital Agency, Government of Japan. I have also introduced some resources provided by other dashboard development platforms that reinforce the development process suggested in the guide. In addition to the resources introduced in this article, there are many other resources from various dashboard development platform vendors. As discussions such as "the materials here are useful" spread on social media, everyone will be able to create more sophisticated dashboards than ever before. I look forward to seeing positive exchange of information.

I hope this blog will be helpful.

# Dashboards 101: Best practices for developing great dashboards

![Picture of Belinda Bailey](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/181c4d84731bc6aac8963237697cea60ec72e49a736b2e775f968cf456f94c82?s=100&d=mm&r=g)
![](https://www.displayr.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/12/dashboard101v2.jpg)

The term 'dashboard' has been adopted from automobiles that display instruments and controls required for the vehicle's operation. For example, a speedometer, so you can check the speed you are driving at, a fuel gauge so you see how much fuel you have, and so on.

We are living in a highly dynamic world and today's business environment is changing rapidly. Market research surveys collect a huge amount of data, and static PowerPoint presentations just can't keep up with the pace. Data-driven dashboards are innovative and trending in market research. They enable you to study your data effortlessly and share a dashboard with a simple link.

A well-designed dashboard report can serve as an effective information management tool and a powerful means of sharing pertinent data at-a-glance. Dashboards hosted online, in particular tracking surveys, are an efficient way to present results to stakeholders or clients. You just need to add the new data, and your dashboard gets automatically updated. There is no need to create a new PowerPoint presentation.

## Dashboards require planning

The planning phase is where it all begins. Once the scope of the dashboard project has been defined, the process of gathering requirements begins. Creating a dashboard that is simple to use and accessible is just one of the obvious requirements for today's forward-thinking businesses.

There are certain dashboard design key principles that you should follow to be sure the dashboard you design really stands out and is visually appealing. Poorly designed dashboards can fail to convey useful information and insights and even make the data less comprehensible.

Before jumping into the design stage of your dashboard project, you need to start with a plan that has clear objectives. Consider the five **Ws**:

**Who**: Who is the intended audience? Make a list of the stakeholders, decision-makers, and end-users of your dashboard.  
**What**: What is the purpose of the dashboard? Create a list of objectives and ensure they are all met.  
**Where**: Where does the data come from? Is it just survey data or in combination with other sources?  
**When**: Does the dashboard show data for one specific time period, or does it require a periodic refresh. If the latter, when, and how frequently should the data be refreshed?  
**Why**: Why do you need to develop the dashboard report?

## Dashboard design principles

Ask yourself, what are the important things to keep in mind when designing a dashboard. On that note, let's enter the world of dashboard design and best practices.

### Consider your audience

One of the most important principles, and the one that is often overlooked when designing a dashboard, is knowing your audience. It starts and ends with your audience. Who is going to use the dashboard? What will the users expect from this dashboard? You should never lose sight of the purpose of designing a dashboard because no one dashboard fits all types of audiences. In the same way that you would design a PowerPoint report, you are designing the dashboard because you want to present the survey data in a clear and concise way. A well-designed dashboard facilitates the decision-making process for the audience in mind. To that end, before diving headfirst into dashboard design, it is a good idea to sit with your end-users, gather requirements, and define the objectives.

From a business intelligence perspective, there are three common types of dashboards:

Outside of the business intelligence realm, dashboard designs can vary enormously and come in all sorts of shapes and sizes. What type of dashboard will work best for your data? The following [blog](https://www.displayr.com/8-types-of-online-dashboards/) post reviews eight types of online dashboards to assist you with choosing the right approach for your next dashboard.

Remember to put yourself in your audience's shoes.

### Tell a clear story

A dashboard provides a snapshot of the data at a specific point in time. The metrics to include in the dashboard will depend on the type of study and objectives of the research. A good dashboard relies on simplicity. The metrics you choose and how you group those metrics should tell a holistic story. Letting the data do the talking is part of good storytelling.

The dashboard should present the findings in an intuitive way making it easy to use and understand. You should avoid clutter or confusing use of color. You don't want to end up losing your audience or cluttering your message.

When designing a dashboard, it’s important to follow some kind of organized structure. Use size and position to show hierarchies and make priorities clear. The inverted pyramid is a metaphor used by journalists to illustrate how information should be prioritized and structured. Simply put, the widest part of the upside-down triangle displays the most important, interesting, or attention-grabbing insights that need to be conveyed. The lower sections give context to these insights, illustrating other data in order of diminishing importance. By using this approach you are leveraging the natural reading pattern to present information in an intuitive manner.

As well as the placement of metrics, you also need to consider the visual consistency of the design, such as fonts, themes, and navigation. Maintaining consistency is absolutely key and equivalent to drafting an intuitive design. One thing is for sure, lack of consistency is likely to lead to a lack of interest!

It is always a good idea to include instructions on how to use the dashboard. Include narrative where necessary explaining what the data might mean. Both of these will support the audience who needs to use the information to make meaningful decisions.

### Use the right data visualization

Choosing the right visualization for your dashboard and making sure your end-user understands what they’re looking at, is another important part of designing a dashboard.  Looking good is not the same as being effective, and visualizing data poorly can become more confusing than helpful.

In the first instance, you need to understand the reasons why you may need to use a particular type of chart. For instance, if you are comparing one or many variable sets, you need to use a comparison chart. Typical comparison charts include a bar, column, pie, line charts, and scatter plots. If you want to show the makeup of a total by category? To show composition you can use a pie chart, stacked bar and column charts, area, and waterfall charts. If you want to understand the distribution of your data, consider using a scatter plot. If you are analyzing trends then line and dual-axis line charts are obvious choices. Finally, if you want to better understand the relationship between two variable sets, you can use a scatter plot or a bubble chart.

## Conclusion

Designing and creating a dashboard report is an iterative and evolving process. It is important to test, evaluate, and tweak your dashboards. User-driven feedback allows you to make your dashboard as effective as possible, and address the specific objectives of your audience.

By using these dashboard design best practices, you’ll guarantee that the dashboard you design will serve as an effective information management tool. In the end, it is about making clients and stakeholders understand their data, empowering them with data-driven insights.
